title: OOP & Design Patterns
description: Python OOP internals and design patterns that appear in FAANG system design rounds and senior engineering interviews. Covers dunder methods, MRO, dataclasses, and the patterns you must know.

sections.title: Dunder Methods — The Full Contract
sections.description:
Dunder (double underscore) methods define how Python objects behave with built-in operations. Getting these right is critical for implementing custom data structures in interviews.

__repr__: unambiguous string for developers (used in REPL/logs). Should ideally be eval-able.
__str__: readable string for end users. Falls back to __repr__ if not defined.
__eq__: equality. Defining it makes Python set __hash__ to None (making object unhashable) unless you define __hash__ too.
__hash__: must be consistent with __eq__. Two equal objects must have the same hash.

sections.title: MRO — Method Resolution Order (C3 Linearization)
sections.description:
In multiple inheritance, Python uses the C3 linearization algorithm to determine method lookup order. Understanding MRO prevents subtle bugs in class hierarchies. Use ClassName.mro() or ClassName.__mro__ to inspect.

Rule: Local class first → left-to-right parent order → depth before breadth, but a class never before its parents.

sections.title: Dataclasses & __slots__
sections.description:
@dataclass auto-generates __init__, __repr__, __eq__ from field annotations. Use frozen=True to get __hash__ and immutability.

__slots__ replaces the per-instance __dict__ with a fixed-size array, saving ~50–200 bytes per instance. Critical when creating millions of objects.
sections.codeBlock:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass(order=True, frozen=True)   # order generates <, >, <=, >=
class Point:
    x: float
    y: float
    label: str = field(default='', compare=False)  # excluded from eq/order

p = Point(1.0, 2.0)
print(p)           # Point(x=1.0, y=2.0, label='')
print(hash(p))     # works because frozen=True → immutable → hashable
{p}                # can be used in sets/dicts

# __slots__ — memory optimization for many instances
class SlottedPoint:
    __slots__ = ('x', 'y')    # NO __dict__ — saves ~200 bytes/instance
    def __init__(self, x, y):
        self.x, self.y = x, y

import sys
p_normal = type('P', (), {'x': 1, 'y': 2})()  # has __dict__
p_slot   = SlottedPoint(1, 2)
print(sys.getsizeof(p_normal.__dict__))  # ~232 bytes
# p_slot has no __dict__ — direct attribute array

sections.title: Abstract Base Classes & Protocols
sections.description: ABC enforces interface contracts at instantiation time — you can't instantiate a class that doesn't implement all abstract methods. Protocol (Python 3.8+) enables structural subtyping (duck typing with static analysis support) — no inheritance needed.

sections.title: Design Patterns — Singleton, Factory, Observer
sections.description:
FAANG system design and senior coding rounds test your knowledge of patterns. Know when to apply each — and when NOT to (overengineering is a red flag).

Singleton: Ensure exactly one instance (DB connection pool, config). Thread-safe via module-level instance or __new__.
Factory: Decouple object creation from usage. Makes code open for extension, closed for modification.
Observer: Event-driven notification. Used in UI frameworks, event buses, reactive systems.

sections.title: Decorators & Context Managers
sections.description: Decorators are higher-order functions that wrap other functions — used for caching, logging, rate limiting, auth. Context managers guarantee resource cleanup — use them for files, DB connections, locks.

details:
Property vs Attribute: Use @property when you need computed values or validation on access — it's a descriptor under the hood. Avoid making simple attributes into properties prematurely (YAGNI). Python's uniform access principle means you can always refactor a plain attribute to a property without changing the API.

Metaclasses: Classes are instances of type (the default metaclass). Custom metaclasses intercept class creation — used for ORMs (Django models), plugin systems, enforcing class-level constraints. Rarely needed in application code; prefer class decorators or ABCs instead.

Interview pattern: When asked to 'design a class', always: (1) define the interface first (what methods should it expose?), (2) choose the right built-in to inherit from or compose, (3) implement dunder methods if needed for collection behavior, (4) discuss thread safety if applicable.
